name: humanity (his creation
shortDescription: In Sumerian mythology, humanity was created by the god Enki (also known as Ea) and the mother goddess Nammu to serve the gods and relieve them of labor. The creation story highlights the relationship between gods and humans in their belief system.
description: In Sumerian mythology, the creation of humanity is attributed primarily to Enki (Ea in Akkadian), the god of wisdom, fresh water, intelligence, and mischief, and to the primordial mother goddess Nammu. The gods, weary of their own labor, desired beings to perform the tasks necessary for the maintenance of the cosmos. Nammu, the goddess of the primeval sea, suggested to her son Enki that he create such beings. Enki, with the aid of other deities, fashioned humanity from clay, imbued with divine essence. The specific details of the creation vary across different texts, but the general narrative involves Enki’s ingenuity in shaping the clay and Nammu’s role in giving birth to the first humans. These humans were intended to serve the gods, providing them with food, shelter, and worship. The myth emphasizes the hierarchical structure of Sumerian society, with the gods at the top and humans in a subservient role. However, the relationship was not purely one-sided; humans also received protection and guidance from the gods. The creation myth reflects the Sumerian understanding of their place in the universe and their relationship with the divine. The tales of early humans and their interactions with the gods are found in various cuneiform texts, including the Atrahasis epic and fragments of other creation myths. The creation of humanity is a central theme in Mesopotamian religion and provides insight into their worldview, ethics, and understanding of the divine-human relationship.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
sources.author: University of Oxford

sources.title: Before Philosophy
sources.author: Henri Frankfort, H.A. Frankfort, John A. Wilson, Thorkild Jacobsen

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Food and Drink
worshipPractices.description: Humans provided sustenance for the gods through regular offerings of food and drink. This was seen as a necessary exchange to ensure the gods' continued support and well-being, thereby maintaining cosmic balance and prosperity for humanity.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Service and Labor
worshipPractices.description: Humans served the gods by maintaining the temples, performing rituals, and engaging in agricultural labor to provide for the temples' needs. This direct service demonstrated devotion and fulfilled the gods' initial purpose in creating humanity.

festivals.name: Akitu Festival
festivals.description: Akitu celebrated the renewal of the earth and the victory of Marduk (a later Mesopotamian adaptation of Enki's role) over chaos. Humans participated through processions, rituals, and symbolic acts to reaffirm the divine order and ensure a prosperous new year.

festivals.name: Sacrifice Rituals
festivals.timing: Specific events and times of need
festivals.description: Sacrifices of animals and, in some instances, humans, were offered to appease the gods and seek their favor during times of crisis, such as famine, war, or plague. These offerings were intended to restore balance and secure divine intervention.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of humanity created to serve a higher power resonates in various religious and philosophical traditions, exploring themes of purpose, responsibility, and the relationship between creators and creations. Sumerian creation myths, particularly those involving Enki and Nammu, continue to inspire artistic interpretations and literary explorations of human origins and our place in the universe, influencing science fiction and fantasy genres.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Prometheus molded humans from clay, defying Zeus to give them fire and knowledge, acting as a benefactor to humanity against the wishes of the ruling god.
Odin and his brothers created the first humans, Ask and Embla, from trees, giving them life, breath, intelligence, and form, populating Midgard.
Nüwa molded humans from yellow earth, creating them to populate the world and repairing the heavens after a great catastrophe, demonstrating care and responsibility for her creation.
Khnum fashioned humans on a potter's wheel from clay, giving them life and form, similar to the Sumerian idea of creation from earth and divine intervention.

associations.name: Clay
associations.significance: Humanity was formed from clay, representing their earthly origins and connection to the physical world.

associations.name: Service
associations.significance: Humanity's primary purpose was to serve the gods, relieving them of burdensome labor.

associations.name: Grain
associations.significance: Humanity cultivates grain, providing sustenance for both themselves and offerings to the gods.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Connected to the creation of humankind in some accounts, seven representing completion or a divine number in Mesopotamian lore.

associations.name: Ingenuity
associations.significance: Reflects Enki's cleverness and his gift of intelligence to humanity.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Symbolizes Enki's domain and the life-giving force that sustains humanity.

associations.name: Mortality
associations.significance: Humanity is mortal and subject to the whims of the gods, a contrast to the gods' immortality.

associations.name: Lamb
associations.significance: Represents the sacrifices offered by humanity to the gods, a symbol of their dependence and devotion.

companions.name: Nammu
companions.description: Nammu is the primordial sea goddess, the mother who gave birth to An (sky) and Ki (earth). She provided the clay from which Enki fashioned humankind, assisting in their creation to relieve the gods' labor.

companions.name: Ninmah
companions.description: Ninmah, a mother goddess, assisted Enki in the creation of humans. She experimented with different forms and qualities, and her involvement shaped the physical and emotional aspects of early humanity.

companions.name: Adapa
companions.description: Adapa was a priest of Ea (Enki), known for his wisdom and loyalty. He was granted immortality by Anu but refused it on Enki's advice, showing the complex relationship between humanity and the divine wisdom bestowed by Enki.

companions.name: Asu
companions.description: Asu were the healers created alongside humans. They are charged with tending to the sicknesses and injuries of humanity, a gift from the gods to ease the burden of mortality.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth: A Response to Divine Weariness
extendedContent.content:
The creation of humanity in Sumerian mythology stems from the gods' dissatisfaction with their own laborious tasks. The gods, responsible for maintaining the cosmic order, found their workload overwhelming. According to various texts, notably the Atrahasis Epic, the Igigi, the lesser gods, rebelled against the Anunnaki, the higher gods, due to the excessive labor demanded of them. This rebellion prompted the Anunnaki to seek a solution: the creation of a servant race to shoulder the burden.

Nammu, the primordial goddess of the watery abyss and mother of all gods, played a crucial role. It was she who proposed to her son Enki, the god of wisdom and fresh water, the idea of creating humans. Enki, renowned for his intelligence and resourcefulness, conceived the plan for their creation. The process involved the mixing of clay with the blood of a slain god, often identified as Geshtu-e, a god embodying intelligence. This divine sacrifice imbued humanity with a spark of divinity, connecting them to the gods and imbuing them with the capacity for reason and understanding.

The Atrahasis Epic details the creation process, describing how the clay was mixed and molded by Nammu and the birth goddesses, known as the Mami. Fourteen 'wombs' were established, resulting in the simultaneous birth of seven male and seven female humans. This deliberate act ensured the rapid propagation of the human race, fulfilling their intended purpose as laborers and worshippers of the gods. This creation narrative underscores the utilitarian origins of humanity within the Sumerian worldview.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Temples: Sustaining the Divine Realm
extendedContent.content:
Humanity's primary purpose, following their creation, was to serve and sustain the gods. This service manifested primarily through worship and the maintenance of temples. Temples, such as the E-Abzu in Eridu dedicated to Enki and the E-Anna in Uruk dedicated to Inanna, were not merely places of worship; they were the economic and administrative centers of Sumerian city-states. Humans cultivated the land surrounding the temples, producing food and resources to be offered to the gods. These offerings were considered essential for the gods' sustenance and well-being, reinforcing the reciprocal relationship between deities and mortals.

Rituals and ceremonies were central to this worship. Priests and priestesses, acting as intermediaries between the human and divine realms, conducted elaborate ceremonies involving hymns, prayers, and sacrifices. The hymns often praised the gods' power and benevolence, while the prayers expressed human needs and desires. Sacrifices, typically of animals or agricultural produce, were offered to appease the gods and ensure their continued favor. The Ziggurats, towering stepped pyramids, served as symbolic mountains connecting the earthly and celestial realms, further emphasizing the importance of temples as points of communication with the divine.

The concept of 'balag,' laments and prayers, highlights the human role in addressing divine displeasure. When faced with natural disasters or societal hardships, Sumerians believed they could appease the gods through these expressions of sorrow and supplication. This illustrates the complex relationship: humans served the gods, but also possessed the agency to influence them through their devotion and expressions of need, showcasing the delicate balance of power and dependence within their religious framework.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation: Humble Servants and Divine Kin
extendedContent.content:
Unlike the gods, who were often depicted in anthropomorphic forms with divine attributes like horned crowns and radiating light, humans were rarely portrayed in Sumerian art with distinct individual features in early periods. Instead, they were typically depicted as smaller figures in scenes of worship, attending to the needs of the gods. Cylinder seals and votive statues often show humans with their hands clasped in prayer, demonstrating their subservient role. These statues were often placed in temples as a perpetual offering of devotion, serving as stand-ins for the individuals who commissioned them.

Later periods, particularly during the Akkadian and Babylonian empires, saw a slight shift in the portrayal of humans. Rulers, such as Sargon of Akkad and Hammurabi, were depicted with more pronounced features, emphasizing their power and authority. However, even these representations maintained a sense of humility before the gods, acknowledging the divine source of their legitimacy. The Stele of Hammurabi, for example, depicts Hammurabi receiving the laws from Shamash, the god of justice, reinforcing the idea that human rulers were ultimately accountable to the divine order.

The absence of distinct iconography for the average human reflects their perceived role as a collective workforce rather than individual actors. Their worth was primarily derived from their contribution to the maintenance of the divine realm, and their representation in art reflected this utilitarian perspective. While humans possessed a spark of divinity through the blood of Geshtu-e, this connection was primarily expressed through their acts of worship and service, rather than through distinct artistic representations of their individual identities.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order: Maintaining Ma'at on Earth
extendedContent.content:
Humanity's creation was intrinsically linked to the maintenance of cosmic order, known as 'me' in Sumerian. The 'me' were fundamental divine decrees and principles that governed all aspects of existence, from social structures to natural phenomena. By diligently performing their duties, humans ensured the smooth functioning of the universe and prevented chaos from engulfing the world. This responsibility extended beyond simple labor; it encompassed ethical behavior, adherence to social norms, and respect for the gods' authority.

Failure to uphold these principles could result in divine punishment, as evidenced by the story of the Great Flood in the Atrahasis Epic. The gods, angered by the noise and wickedness of humanity, decided to wipe them out with a devastating deluge. Only Atrahasis, a wise and righteous king, was warned by Enki and instructed to build an ark to save himself, his family, and representatives of all living creatures. This narrative highlights the precarious position of humanity and their dependence on the gods' favor for their survival.

Ultimately, humanity's role in the cosmic order was one of stewardship. They were tasked with cultivating the land, building temples, and worshipping the gods, thereby contributing to the stability and prosperity of the world. While they were subordinate to the gods, their actions had significant consequences for the well-being of the entire cosmos. This understanding of humanity's place in the universe shaped their worldview and guided their social and religious practices.

subtitle: Born from divine weariness, to serve and till the earth.
